speaker: interviewer
text: --the 8, 1995, Brooklyn, New York, interviewer Rickey Halperin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Hungary. And I really became literally sick when these youngsters put on the music. I realized, you know, there's a generation gap here. They didn't go through this. But still, they should know what this is. And they finally listened and stopped the music. And I told them, these are cities with a vibrant Jewish life. There were tens of thousands of beautiful boys and girls singing, running around, living a decent life, a productive life. And they were all murdered in these cities. What we should really do is to go into every city and every town and scream and recite the eulogy, what in Hebrew we call a [NON-ENGLISH]. Not an ordinary eulogy, but a bitter eulogy, and to tell all these people who their parents and grandparents were, and possibly they themselves, murderers. They should know what happened. This is not the place for rejoicing. And from then on, we did not enjoy ourselves at all. We realized the seriousness and the trauma [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of all these places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did something I don't usually do. It's the first time I went back after the-- after Bergen-Belsen. Every city I went by, I cursed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used Talmudic expression. First, with all these towns and villages [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are so broken up, in such disrepair. They killed the Jews. And there's no one to do anything there. It's like a medieval country. And I said that this should be [NON-ENGLISH] It should be a ruin forever. [NON-ENGLISH] It should never, ever be rebuilt. In Yiddish I said, [YIDDISH] And the sun should never shine upon them. And the whole bus said, [NON-ENGLISH] at every one of these stops. I took this precedent from our King David, who cursed the Mountain of Gilboa. Because King Saul was killed upon that. And when we left Hungary, for some reason, I lost my passport. And everybody came back. And I stayed one more day, which was quite traumatic. I thought, really, I'm back in '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, I obtained the second passport. And I even found my first one. I come to the airport next day. I see they're putting me on the Malev Airline with 300 Hungarians, non-Jews, to come here. It didn't look good to me. But still, I started to speak to people. I introduced myself [INAUDIBLE] back for the first time in 50 years. And I-- and they say, well, we were forced to do this, this, that. I told them, no, it's not true. I said, I remember the faces. Everybody did this with a smile and laugh and dancing. They were happy to do this to us. Nobody forced anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then as I sit there, I sat in a hat. I take off my hat, and I put on my yarmulke. So a lady comes-- runs over to me. And about a few hundred people there. This was a huge waiting room. She says, listen, I'm a Jewish lady from Detroit. Put back your hat. This-- you are just rousing the ire of these anti-Semites.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I [? need it ?] over there. And I got up. And I screamed out loud. I wanted to make sure that it's obvious to everyone that I'm a Jew. I'm proud to be a Jew. And I do not come from a nation of murderers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy I said this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like a climax to a story that started in 1940. I went back. And I let them know how I feel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later, a lady who introduced herself also as a Jewish lady from New York came over and congratulated me. She said she's proud. I think that was a fitting end to the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up to about 15 years ago, probably, I had nightmares. I dreamt many times that I'm being deported. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had many nightmares and frequently. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Not so much lately. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But now, I am much more active in sharing these experiences. I speak a lot all over the United States. Specifically, I spend a week every year in Minneapolis. I speak there to the high schools and to the neighboring universities, like St. Olaf and Carleton, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to various Jewish communities and schools. I wish some of our schools would expose the kids more to this. There's no reason. This happened to our people, not to some other people. There's absolutely no excuse for a Jewish school not to have this as a prime topic of-- of their curriculum. There's no excuse for that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's with you. It's constantly with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sometimes, the happy moments you contrast. One of the most beautiful moments I think I had my life in connection with the Holocaust was in 1968. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was the 20th anniversary of the State of Israel, [NON-ENGLISH]. I bought [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my family specifically tickets to witness the parade of the Israel army. It was the first time they paraded in Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was probably the most beautiful, emotional experience I ever had in my life. I contrasted every second of that to our weakness and helplessness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say utter helplessness, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I thank God. I pray to God every second, every moment, as I witnessed that parade, thanking God. I said, God-- there is a God. God is here. That was the most obvious show of partiality of God to His people. I speak a lot about the subject of what would be if, God forbid, after the Shoah, after the Holocaust, there would not have been the Jewish State. I doubt people think about it, especially all these big shots who criticize the State of Israel. My answer is, there would be no Jewish people today. It would have been impossible for us to justify our existence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We say this prayer during the high holidays during the Mussaf service. We ask God, [NON-ENGLISH] God give honor to us, [NON-ENGLISH] and hope to those who fear You. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we also use these two words that people just gloss over. But I think it's very significant. We say, [NON-ENGLISH], that those who hope in you, God, enable them to justify their hope in You. If there would have been no Jewish State, we'd have said, well, once there was a Jewish people. There was a God. And He killed his people. And He said goodbye to them. That's exactly how it would happen. We might have had-- have a handful of Jewish people today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The establishment of the State [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] enables us to exist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God came and showed again on whose side he is. And we believe that all this is part of the Messianic process, including the Holocaust. And we believe, of course, in the resurrection of the dead [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the culmination is the arrival of the Messiah. This is myself at about the age of three in Szeged and the first time with a book. Here I am with my younger sister, Esther. I believe she is probably about two. And I'm probably about five.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather on my father's side, the Rabbi Yisroel Ha Levi Jungreis. He was killed in Auschwitz. [INAUDIBLE]. This is his wife. Her name is Slova Chane Jungreis. And she was the [INAUDIBLE]. She was also killed in Auschwitz. This is my grandfather on my mother's side, Rabbi Tzvi Hersch Kohn, the Rabbi of Derecske. He was the rabbi in Vienna during the Shoah. And he survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is his wife, Rabbitzin Rivka Kohn of Derecske. She was killed in Auschwitz. This is my father, Rabbi Abraham Jungreis, as a student in the world-renowned Yeshiva Rabbinical Academy of Pressburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt, my father's sister, Elsa Junreis [INAUDIBLE]. She's the one who put us on the list for the Kasztner train. And she eventually lived in Israel. This my uncle, Rabbi Moshe Kohn, who was killed in the Ukraines while clearing a mine field where all the Jewish soldiers are sent first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt, [INAUDIBLE] Jungreis, my father's sister. She was killed in the Shoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: One moment. OK, the middle one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Rabbitzin Miriam Jungreisz. Thank God, she survived the Shoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, then the last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt, my mother's sister, [? Zelda ?] Kohn. She was killed in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken the DP camp in Switzerland in 1945. From left to right, my sister Esther, my mother, myself, and my little brother, [INAUDIBLE] Binyomin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the middle one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is also taken in Switzerland. This is one of the boys that we tried to draw close to, Jewish-- Yiddish guide. I forgot his name. And next to him stands my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also in the DP camp in Switzerland. Left to right, first is myself, my father, and my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document depicts me as a stateless person. It was given by the Swiss government. It says that I'm-- to use their words-- [NON-ENGLISH], which is almost like being imprisoned. I do not have total freedom of movement. And I-- I'm allowed to live only where I am given permission to live. This was taken soon after we came from Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my naturalization paper. It made me officially a citizen of the United States, which entitled me, for the first time in many, many years, or probably first time in my life, to a genuine, real passport. And after this, I was not called a stateless person. And since then, I always live with a passport. I am never, ever without one. Since the Shoah, we have always felt a Jew should always have to have a passport on him. One never knows what happens. We should only need it for happy occasions. If there's anything that the Shoah taught us it's that we need our own state, or own country, where we belong and we don't get kicked around. And since Bergen-Belsen, my family became ardent Zionists. And here, we have a picture of myself, my sister, my brother saluting the flag of Israel. This is my wife, Shifra, although she's an American girl. But she shared with me the Holocaust in every intimate detail, A to Z. And this is our older son, Binyomin Yitchak. And he's also aware of the entire pain and struggle of Jewish history. And this is our granddaughter, [? Sarah ?] [? Eliza ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And [? Sarah Eliza ?] was named after Elsa, who put us on the Kasztner list, which saved our lives. And this is our youngest, Tzvi Hersch. And I am very proud of him. Even at his young age, he is very aware of the Shoah. He knows every detail of it. Mrs, Jungreis, is there anything you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: As an American, I, of course, was not that aware. Although, my parents did participate in all the rallies and all the protests, living here in America. I think the first time it was brought home to me was when my very law-abiding parents decided to smuggle guns to Israel to help the fight there for independence. Because they were frustrated at their inability to help. My parents told me that when they first-- when General Eisenhower liberated the camps and it was on the front page of the General Eisenhower liberated the camps and the pictures were on the front page of the That's when it really hit home. And they were not fully aware, no matter how much they thought they were. And they were determined that would do whatever is in their power to see to it that it didn't happen again. And all of sudden, I turned around, and I see guns all over the house. They had collected them from returning servicemen and were sending it to Israel. As far as my husband is concerned, to this day, we sleep with 100 watt light in the room. It took me quite a while to get used to it. And the first two years of our marriage, he had nightmares almost every night. That has more or less dissipated, except if something does startle him, even a slight sound, he will call out and cry. So in essence, it lives with us almost every single day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys, would you like to say anything or add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitchak.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I think about the Holocaust, I would say a number of times each and every single day. It's-- it's very difficult being quite a number of years removed it and being really an American in almost every single way. It's-- it's difficult to comprehend what happened to the Jews of Europe. And it's something that haunts me. I-- I would say, every single day, I think about it quite a number of times. And it's also something that I have absolutely no explanation for. A lot of things I'd like to discuss, because I think I can shed light on the subject and offer insights or explanation. The Holocaust is something that's totally beyond me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could just say that as a boy growing up in the house of a Holocaust survivor, I remember going to the Museum of Modern Art when I was, I think, 9, 10 years old. And there was a picture of train tracks that led to nowhere. And the first thing I immediately thought of was the Holocaust. I don't think the average 10-year-old kid in America seeing those train tracks-- the-- the most far-fetched thing from his mind would be the Holocaust. And also, even if I have nowhere to go as a result of the Holocaust, I always make sure that I have a valid passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Jungreis, could you end with a prayer or some words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I will tell you, in the Talmudic times, they had a debate as to how can you prove that there's a God in the world? And that's not so easy to prove it on this planet. And they said, well, look at the sun. If you could see into it, and if you could open your eyes and look into it, you know, that's impossible. So if that is just a creation of God and you cannot see it, so how can you see God? I said that was 2,000 years ago. Today, I have an obvious proof. I am very religious. I am very deep believer in God as a result of the Holocaust. And I have always an obvious proof that is God in the world. About 2 o'clock in the afternoons, usually I drive down towards the ocean near Kennedy Airport. And about that time where an airplane comes in from Israel about to land. And I stop the car. And I say [NON-ENGLISH] This proves that there is a God in the world. It's the plane with the Magen David, the Star of David, a Jewish pilot speaking Hebrew, and coming from our homeland, from the land that we live there. We are independent there. And a Jew is welcome there, and that it proves that there is a God in the world who, after all, watches over His people. And at the weakest moment of history, God revealed himself to us. And we hope with the help of God, we will soon see the final redemption of the Jewish people. And we do believe in the resurrection. And all these people will come back. And we will meet everyone who was so brutally murdered once more very soon. Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, we'd like to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for listening.
